```mermaid
    Q4 --> U1[Cognizable vs Non Cognizable]
    Q4 --> U2[Bailable vs Non Bailable]
    Q4 --> U3[Compoundable vs Non Compoundable]
```

## 4.3 Six-Step Legal Mapping Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1: Identify the Guilty Act] --> stepB[Step 2: Match to IT Act Section]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Cross Check with IPC]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Identify Forum and Jurisdiction]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Quantify Damages and Remedy]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Ensure Procedural Compliance with CERT In]
    stepF --> stepG[Outcome: Prosecution plus Compensation plus Compliance]
```

## 4.4 Security-Control Layered Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    layer1[Layer 1: Perimeter Security] --> layer2[Layer 2: Network Security]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3: Application Security]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer 4: Data Security]
    layer1 --> ctrl1[Firewall plus IDS plus IPS plus DDoS Scrubber]
    layer2 --> ctrl2[VPN plus Segmentation plus 802.1X]
    layer3 --> ctrl3[Input Validation plus WAF plus Parameterized Queries]
    layer4 --> ctrl4[AES 256 plus TLS 1.3 plus HSM]
    ctrl1 --> legal1[Legal Basis: IT Act Section 69]
    ctrl2 --> legal2[Legal Basis: IT Act Section 43-A Reasonable Security]
    ctrl3 --> legal3[Legal Basis: IT Act Section 66 Damage to System]
    ctrl4 --> legal4[Legal Basis: IT Act Section 72-A plus DPDP Act 2023]
```

## 4.5 CERT-In 6-Hour Reporting Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    inc[Cyber Incident Detected] --> tri[Triage within 1 hour]
    tri --> class{Is it a Reportable Incident Type?}
    class -- Yes --> log[Log with timestamp and evidence]
    class -- No --> handle[Handle Internally and Document]
    log --> cert[Report to CERT In within 6 hours]
    cert --> inv[Investigation and Forensic Preservation]
    inv --> closure[Closure Report within 30 days]
    handle --> closure
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, April 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define cyber security. Mention any two legal provisions in the IT Act 2000 that deal with unauthorized access.
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Cyber security is the practice of protecting systems, networks, programs, devices, and data from digital attacks. It encompasses technical, legal, and procedural safeguards. Two relevant legal provisions are:

1. **Section 66** of the IT Act, 2000 — *Computer-related offences* — punishes unauthorized access, downloading, extracting, or copying data with imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹5 lakh**.
2. **Section 43** of the IT Act, 2000 — *Penalty for damage to computer system* — imposes a civil liability (compensation up to **₹1 crore**) on any person who accesses a computer without permission.

---

### Q2. What is cyber terrorism? Which section of the IT Act deals with it and what is the punishment?
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Cyber terrorism is the act of using computer resources to threaten the unity, integrity, sovereignty, economic security, or strike terror in the people of a nation. It is covered under **Section 66-F** of the IT Act, 2000 (inserted by the 2008 amendment). The punishment is **imprisonment which may extend to life imprisonment**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, ESE Pattern]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q A (a).** Explain in detail the **CIA Triad** of cyber security. Map each component to the **relevant section of the IT Act, 2000** and describe the **technological controls** used to enforce it. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security:

1. **Confidentiality** — Ensures that data is accessible *only* to those authorized to view it.
   - *Technology:* AES-256 encryption, Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), Data Loss Prevention (DLP) systems.
   - *Legal mapping:* **Section 72** of the IT Act, 2000 prescribes imprisonment up to **2 years** and fine up to **₹1 lakh** for breach of confidentiality by a service provider.

2. **Integrity** — Ensures that data remains *unaltered* except by authorized actors.
   - *Technology:* SHA-256 hashing, digital signatures (under **Section 3** of the IT Act), version control, audit logs.
   - *Legal mapping:* **Section 66** of the IT Act covers damage to computer systems and data, with imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹5 lakh**.

3. **Availability** — Ensures that systems and data are *accessible* on demand.
   - *Technology:* Redundant servers, load balancers, DDoS mitigation, off-site backups, disaster recovery (DR) sites.
   - *Legal mapping:* **Section 66-F** (cyber terrorism), which criminalizes denial-of-service attacks intended to threaten national sovereignty, with punishment of **life imprisonment**.

The triad is *non-negotiable*: a breach in any one of the three compromises the entire security posture.

---

**Q A (b).** Discuss the **classification of cyber crimes** along the four axes of target, actor, modus operandi, and legal character. Provide **two examples** for each axis. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Cyber crimes can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Target**
   - *Person:* Cyber stalking (IT Act §354-D IPC), online harassment.
   - *Property:* Hacking, phishing, ransomware.
   - *Government:* Cyber terrorism (§66-F), cyber espionage (§69).

2. **By Actor**
   - *Insider:* Disgruntled employee stealing customer data (§72, §43-A).
   - *External:* Organized crime syndicates, state-sponsored APT groups.

3. **By Modus Operandi**
   - *Technical:* SQL injection, malware, zero-day exploits, ransomware.
   - *Social:* Phishing (§66-D), vishing, smishing, baiting, pretexting.

4. **By Legal Character (under CrPC / IT Act)**
   - *Cognizable vs Non-Cognizable:* Most IT Act offences (e.g., §66) are **cognizable**, allowing arrest without warrant.
   - *Bailable vs Non-Bailable:* §66-F (cyber terrorism) is **non-bailable**.
   - *Compoundable vs Non-Compoundable:* §66-D is **non-compoundable**, meaning only the State can compound the offence.

This four-axis classification helps investigators, prosecutors, and corporate compliance teams to map incidents to the *correct statutory remedy* and *correct forum*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - Students often list the *three* axes (target, actor, method) but **omit the fourth axis (legal character)**. This costs **2 marks**.
> - Always cite the **specific Section number** (e.g., §66-D), not just the *name* of the offence. Section-number-blind answers lose **1 mark per occurrence**.
> - Do **not** confuse *Cognizable* (police can arrest) with *Compoundable* (victim can settle). These are independent classifications — a crime can be cognizable *and* non-compoundable.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**Q B (a).** Explain the **legal framework for cyber security in India** with reference to the **IT Act 2000, the IT (Amendment) Act 2008, and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023**. Discuss the role of **CERT-In** in ensuring national cyber security. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. The IT Act, 2000 (Original)**
- Provided the *first* legal recognition to electronic records, digital signatures, and e-commerce.
- Established **Sections 43, 65, 66** for unauthorized access, tampering, and computer-related offences.

**2. The IT (Amendment) Act, 2008**
- Inserted **Sections 66-A to 66-F**, including:
  - §66-A (struck down by *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India*, 2015) — sending offensive messages.
  - §66-B — dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource.
  - §66-C — identity theft.
  - §66-D — cheating by personation (phishing).
  - §66-E — violation of privacy.
  - §66-F — cyber terrorism.
- Strengthened the role of **CERT-In** under **Section 70-B**.

**3. The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)**
- Imposes obligations on **Data Fiduciaries** to implement *reasonable security safeguards* (Section 8).
- Penalties up to **₹250 crore** for failure to protect personal data.
- Establishes the **Data Protection Board of India** for adjudication.

**4. Role of CERT-In (Computer Emergency Response Team — India)**
- Designated as the **national agency for incident response** under §70-B.
- Issued the **Directions of 28 April 2022**, mandating:
  - **6-hour** reporting of cyber incidents.
  - Synchronization of system clocks with NTP.
  - Maintenance of **logs for 180 days**.
- Empowered to call for information and issue advisories.

---

**Q B (b).** Describe the **types of cyber attacks** — phishing, ransomware, and DDoS. For each, identify (i) the **IT Act section** applicable, (ii) the **technology control** to mitigate, and (iii) the **penalty / punishment** prescribed. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Phishing**
- *Description:* Fraudulent emails or websites that impersonate legitimate entities to steal credentials or money.
- *IT Act Section:* **§66-D** — Cheating by personation using communication device.
- *Technology Control:* Email filtering (DMARC, DKIM, SPF), user awareness training, MFA.
- *Punishment:* Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine.

**2. Ransomware**
- *Description:* Malware that encrypts the victim's data and demands a ransom for the decryption key.
- *IT Act Section:* **§43** (damage) read with **§66** (computer-related offence) and **§66-F** (if critical infrastructure is targeted, treating it as cyber terrorism).
- *Technology Control:* Regular offline backups, endpoint detection and response (EDR), network segmentation, patch management.
- *Punishment:* Up to **3 years** (§66) or **life imprisonment** (§66-F in extreme cases).

**3. Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)**
- *Description:* Overwhelming a target server with traffic from multiple compromised systems to disrupt service.
- *IT Act Section:* **§66-F** if the attack threatens sovereignty / economic security; otherwise **§66** for system disruption.
- *Technology Control:* DDoS scrubbing services (e.g., Akamai, Cloudflare), rate limiting, anycast routing.
- *Punishment:* **Life imprisonment** (§66-F) or up to **3 years** (§66).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B Pitfall:**
> - For *ransomware*, students often cite **only §66** and **forget to escalate to §66-F** for critical-infrastructure attacks. This costs **2 marks**.
> - For *DDoS*, mentioning **rate limiting** alone is insufficient — you must also mention **upstream scrubbing**, as DDoS attacks saturate the *last-mile link*.
> - The DPDP Act 2023 question is *new* in the 2024 scheme; examiners will award **bonus attention** to students who cite **Section 8** of the DPDP Act specifically (reasonable security safeguards).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability. Memorize one IT Act section per leg: **§72 / §66 / §66-F**.
- [ ] **AAA Framework** = Authentication + Authorization + Accounting (for access control).
- [ ] **Four axes of cyber-crime classification**: target, actor, modus operandi, legal character.
- [ ] **Phishing → §66-D**; **Identity theft → §66-C**; **Cyber terrorism → §66-F (life imprisonment)**.
- [ ] **§43** is *civil* (compensation up to **₹1 crore**); **§66** is *criminal* (up to **3 years** + ₹5 lakh).
- [ ] **CERT-In** = national incident response agency under **§70-B**; reports must be filed within **6 hours** (2022 Directions).
- [ ] **DPDP Act 2023** = Data Protection Board + penalties up to **₹250 crore** for breach of personal data.
- [ ] **§3 of IT Act** = legal recognition of **digital signatures** (using asymmetric crypto + hash function).
- [ ] **Budapest Convention (2001)** = first international treaty on cybercrime (India is *not* a signatory but follows the principles).
- [ ] **"Reasonable security practice"** under **§43-A** is benchmarked against **ISO/IEC 27001**.
- [ ] **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CyAT)** under **§48** hears appeals; abolished for *new* matters by the Finance Act 2017 but existing matters are transferred to **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)**.
- [ ] **Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)** struck down **§66-A** of the IT Act for violating Article 19(1)(a) — *freedom of speech*.
- [ ] **Adjudicating Officer** under **§46** handles claims up to **₹5 crore**.
- [ ] **Cryptography** is *not* banned in India; the IT Act permits "reasonable" encryption but the Telegraph Act and licensing rules historically restricted *cryptanalysis tools* (note: today, the *Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021* + CERT-In Directions govern).
- [ ] **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023** is the *latest* data-protection law — supersedes the 2018 draft.
- [ ] **Risk = Likelihood × Impact** — use this 2×2 matrix in any KTU answer asking for "prioritization of cyber threats".

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Every technical control has a legal twin. Memorize the twin."* — A KTU 2024 Topper's Rule of Thumb.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intellectual Property Aspects in Cyber Law and Evidence Aspects in Cyber Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST419 - CYBER ETHICS, PRIVACY AND LEGAL ISSUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intellectual Property Aspects in Cyber Law and Evidence Aspects in Cyber Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intellectual Property (IP) in Cyber Law

### Formal Definition
**Intellectual Property (IP)** refers to the legal rights that arise from creations of the human mind and intellect. In the context of cyber law, IP encompasses the protection of intangible digital assets — including software code, digital content, website designs, domain names, databases, algorithms, and multimedia works — through statutes like the **Copyright Act, 1957**, the **Patents Act, 1970**, the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**, and the **Information Technology Act, 2000**.

In cyber law, IP is the bridge that converts intangible digital creativity into legally enforceable ownership, allowing creators to control, monetize, and defend their work in the borderless digital ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the IT Act 2000, **Sections 65 (Tampering with Computer Source Documents)** and **Section 66 (Computer Related Offences)** directly criminalize unauthorized use, reproduction, or distribution of copyrighted software and digital content.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of Intellectual Property like a **"digital fence"** around a farm you built on rented cloud land. You may not own the underlying land (the internet platform, the server, the protocol), but the fence (legal rights) protects everything you grow inside — your code, your brand, your designs. If someone jumps the fence and steals your harvest (your content, your software, your logo), cyber law gives you the right to file a complaint and recover damages.

In simpler terms:
- **Copyright** = The recipe of the dish you invented.
- **Patent** = The unique cooking machine you invented.
- **Trademark** = The logo/name of your restaurant.
- **Trade Secret** = The special spice mix only your chef knows.
- **Domain Name** = The street address of your restaurant online.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric / Key Statistic:** Globally, software piracy causes losses exceeding **$46 billion USD annually** (BSA Global Software Survey estimates), and India ranks among the top consumers of pirated software. This is why IP enforcement in cyberspace is a critical topic under KTU Module 1.

---

## 1.2 Evidence Aspects in Cyber Law (Electronic Evidence)

### Formal Definition
**Electronic Evidence** (also called *Digital Evidence* or *Cyber Evidence*) refers to any information of probative value that is generated, stored, transmitted, or processed in electronic or digital form and is presented in a court of law to establish the truth of a matter. In India, electronic evidence is primarily governed by the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (as amended by the IT Act, 2000)**, specifically through the insertion of **Section 65A and Section 65B**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine traditional evidence (a signed paper contract, a bloodstained knife, a fingerprint) as **physical puzzle pieces**. Electronic evidence, in contrast, is like **digital puzzle pieces** — fragments of data hidden inside servers, mobile phones, log files, email headers, RAM dumps, and cloud storage. The challenge is that these pieces:
1. Are invisible to the naked eye.
2. Can be deleted or altered in milliseconds.
3. Are easy to fake.

Therefore, the law imposes **strict admissibility rules** (Section 65B certificate requirement) and **chain of custody protocols** so that courts can trust what they "see" in digital form — just like a forensic lab fingerprints a knife before letting it into the courtroom.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The landmark case **Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014)** by the Supreme Court of India made it **mandatory** to produce a **Section 65B(4) certificate** for any electronic record to be admissible as secondary evidence. This single judgment transformed cyber evidence practice in India.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Topics Are Studied Together

| Dimension | Intellectual Property | Electronic Evidence |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Legal Domain** | Substantive Law (defines rights) | Procedural Law (defines proof) |
| **Primary Statute** | Copyright Act 1957, IT Act 2000 | Indian Evidence Act 1872 (amended) |
| **Core Question** | *Who owns this digital creation?* | *Can this digital artifact be trusted in court?* |
| **Cyber Linkage** | Software, content, domains, code | Emails, logs, hard disks, cloud data |
| **Conflict Resolution** | Injunctions, damages, criminal prosecution | Admissibility, authenticity, chain of custody |

In any cybercrime trial — say, a software piracy case or a domain name dispute — both aspects operate simultaneously: IP law defines **what was violated**, and evidence law determines **how the violation is proven**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Intellectual Property in Cyber Law

### A. Copyright in Cyberspace
**Copyright** is an automatic, exclusive right granted to the creator of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works — including **computer programs, databases, and digital content**.

**Key Provisions Applicable to Cyber Law:**

| Section of Copyright Act | Offence | Penalty |
|--------------------------|---------|---------|
| Section 63 | Infringement of copyright | Min 6 months imprisonment, ₹50,000–₹2,00,000 fine |
| Section 65 | Tampering with CMIs (Copyright Management Information) | Up to 2 years imprisonment + fine |
| Section 65A | Circumvention of technological protection measures | Up to 2 years imprisonment + fine |

> [!NOTE]
> **Computer Programs** are classified as "literary works" under **Section 2(o)** of the Copyright Act, 1957 — meaning source code and object code both enjoy copyright protection.

**KTU-Relevant Concepts:**
1. **Idea vs. Expression Dichotomy** — Copyright protects *expression*, not the underlying *idea*. A software's logic/algorithm may not be protected, but the actual code is.
2. **Fair Use Doctrine (Section 52)** — Permits limited use for research, criticism, review, news reporting, and non-commercial educational purposes.
3. **Digital Rights Management (DRM)** — Technical locks (encryption, license keys, watermarking) used by copyright holders to control digital use.
4. **Software Piracy** — Unauthorised copying, distribution, or use of software. Includes:
   - **Softlifting** — Copying for friends/colleagues.
   - **Hard-disk loading** — Pre-installing pirated software on computers for sale.
   - **Counterfeiting** — Duplicating and selling software as genuine.
   - **Online piracy** — Torrent sharing, cracking via warez sites.

---

### B. Patents in Cyberspace
A **Patent** is an exclusive right granted for a novel invention, providing the inventor a 20-year monopoly. In cyber law, patents cover:
- Software algorithms (subject to Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, which excludes "computer programs per se").
- Business methods (controversial — see *U.S. State Street Bank* vs *Amazon One-Click* debate).
- Cryptographic methods.
- Hardware-software integrated inventions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Position on Software Patents:** Under **Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970**, "a computer program per se" is **not patentable**. However, if the software produces a "technical effect" or is integrated with hardware, it may be patentable. Example: A novel image compression algorithm running on specialized hardware.

---

### C. Trademarks & Domain Names
**Trademarks** identify the source of goods/services. In cyberspace, trademark disputes center on **domain name conflicts**.

**Cybersquatting:** Registering, trafficking in, or using a domain name identical or confusingly similar to another's trademark with **bad faith intent to profit**.

**Indian Legal Remedy:** **The Information Technology Act, 2000 (Section 67–68)** and the **.IN Dispute Resolution Policy (INDRP)** by NIXI (National Internet Exchange of India) provide remedies.

**Notable Precedents:**
- *Yahoo! Inc. v. Akash Arora* (1999, Delhi HC) — "yahooindia.com" restrained as passing off.
- *Satyam Infoway v. Sifynet Solutions* (2004, SC) — Domain names as trademarks under Indian law.

---

### D. Trade Secrets in the Digital Age
A **trade secret** is confidential business information that provides competitive edge (formulas, algorithms, customer lists, source code). Protection under:
- **The Indian Contract Act, 1872** (breach of confidentiality clauses).
- **The Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 66, 66E for unauthorized access).
- **Common law action for breach of confidence.**

The famous case **E.I. du Pont de Nemours & Co. v. Christopher (1970, U.S.)** established that trade secret theft via photography was actionable even without physical trespass — a foundational principle for cyber espionage cases.

---

### E. Designs and Semiconductor IP
- **Designs Act, 2000** — Protects the visual aesthetic of an article (icons, GUIs, layouts).
- **Semiconductor IC Layout Design Act, 2004** — Protects the 3D configuration of integrated circuits.

---

## 2.2 Electronic Evidence — Deep Theoretical Framework

### A. Types of Digital Evidence
| Type | Examples | Volatility |
|------|----------|-----------|
| **Volatile Evidence** | RAM contents, running processes, network connections, ARP cache | Lost when power off (seconds) |
| **Non-Volatile Evidence** | Hard disk files, USB data, SSD storage | Persistent |
| **Network Evidence** | Firewall logs, packet captures, IDS alerts | Rolling/overwritten |
| **Cloud Evidence** | SaaS data, IaaS logs, email servers | Third-party controlled |
| **Mobile Evidence** | SMS, GPS logs, app data, call records | Easily wiped remotely |

### B. Admissibility Framework — Indian Evidence Act

**Before IT Act 2000 Amendment:**
- Electronic records were considered *hearsay* and *secondary evidence* — frequently rejected.

**After IT Act 2000 Amendment (Sections 65A, 65B, 67, 73A, 131, 134):**
- **Section 65A** — Special provisions for admissibility of electronic records.
- **Section 65B(1)** — Any information contained in an electronic record which is printed on a paper, stored, recorded or copied in optical or magnetic media shall be deemed to be also a document, and shall be admissible.
- **Section 65B(2)** — Conditions for admissibility (4 cumulative conditions).
- **Section 65B(4)** — Certificate requirement (the most important clause).

### C. The Four Conditions of Section 65B(2)
A digital record is admissible **only if** ALL four conditions are satisfied:

1. The electronic record containing the information was produced by a computer **during the period over which the computer was used regularly** to store or process information for the purposes of any lawful activity.
2. The information was **fed into the computer in the ordinary course of the activities** of the person in lawful custody of the record.
3. The computer was **operating properly** or, if not, any malfunction did not affect either the accuracy of the information or the integrity of the record.
4. The information contained in the electronic record **reproduces or is derived from** the information fed into the computer in the ordinary course.

### D. The Section 65B(4) Certificate
A certificate stating the **above four conditions**, signed by a person occupying a responsible official position in relation to the operation of the relevant device — called a **Certifying Officer**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse who can issue the certificate. It is **NOT** the investigating officer. It must be the person in *lawful custody/operation* of the device — e.g., the system administrator, the IT head of the company, or the forensic analyst who extracted the data.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Concept | Statutory Reference | Key Rule | Practical Implication |
|---------|---------------------|----------|------------------------|
| Software = Literary Work | Copyright Act §2(o) | Source & object code protected | Code theft = infringement |
| Idea vs Expression | Copyright Act §13(1)(a) | Expression protected, idea not | Re-implementing logic ≠ infringement |
| Fair Use | Copyright Act §52 | 7 specific exceptions | Education, research, criticism allowed |
| TPM Bypass | Copyright Act §65A | Circumvention = offence | DRM cracking is criminal |
| Software Patent | Patents Act §3(k) | "per se" excluded | Must show technical effect |
| Cybersquatting | IT Act §67–68 | Bad faith domain registration | INDRP arbitration available |
| Trade Secret | Contract Act + IT Act §66 | Confidential info protected | Non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) |
| Admissibility | Evidence Act §65B | 4 conditions + certificate | Mandatory for digital proof |
| Certifying Officer | Evidence Act §65B(4) | Person in lawful operation | NOT the IO/police |
| Electronic Signature | IT Act §3–10A | Asymmetric crypto + PKI | Legal validity assured |
| Search & Seizure | IT Act §80 | Warrant for digital devices | Forensic imaging required |
| Expert Opinion | Evidence Act §45 | Digital forensic expert | Authenticates complex data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Quick Tip:** For any 14-mark question on evidence, always structure your answer around the **4 conditions of Section 65B(2)** + **65B(4) Certificate** + **Relevant Supreme Court Case Law** (Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer, Arjun Panditrao v. Kailash Kushanrao 2020).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Software Companies** rely on copyright + trade secrets to protect proprietary source code (e.g., TCS, Infosys proprietary platforms).
2. **E-commerce Platforms** use trademarks + domain laws to fight counterfeiting and phishing sites.
3. **Cybersecurity Incident Response Teams (CSIRTs)** follow chain-of-custody protocols under Section 65B to ensure forensic evidence holds up in court.
4. **AI/ML Engineers** must understand IP because training data may have copyrighted content — the *Authors Guild v. OpenAI* lawsuit (2023) is a global reference.
5. **Blockchain Developers** must consider how on-chain evidence interacts with Section 65B admissibility (a frontier area of cyber law).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of IP Forms

### Step 1 — Identifying the IP Type

When a digital asset is created, the first analytical step is to classify the IP correctly:

$$\text{Digital Asset} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Code/Software} \rightarrow \text{Copyright (auto)} + \text{Possible Patent (if technical effect)} \\ \text{Brand/Logo/Domain} \rightarrow \text{Trademark (registered or common law)} \\ \text{Confidential Algorithm} \rightarrow \text{Trade Secret (via NDA)} \\ \text{UI/Icon Design} \rightarrow \text{Design Registration} \\ \text{Integrated Circuit Layout} \rightarrow \text{Semiconductor IP} \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Determining the Protection Available

For each IP type, calculate the *protection strength* using:

$$\text{Protection Strength (P)} = f(\text{Statutory Coverage}, \text{Evidence Strength}, \text{Enforcement Cost})$$

In practical terms:
- **Copyright:** High statutory coverage, moderate evidence (easy to prove copying), low enforcement cost (civil + criminal).
- **Patent:** Highest protection strength, but requires registration, technical examination, high cost.
- **Trademark:** Strong if registered; weak if only common-law rights.
- **Trade Secret:** Indefinite but vulnerable if independently reverse-engineered.

### Step 3 — Determining the Cause of Action

If a violation occurs, the analytical flow is:

$$\text{Violation Detected} \rightarrow \text{Identify IP Type} \rightarrow \text{Apply Correct Statute} \rightarrow \text{Choose Forum} \rightarrow \text{Sue or Prosecute}$$

### Step 4 — Forum Selection Matrix

| IP Type | Civil Forum | Criminal Forum | Alternative Dispute Resolution |
|---------|------------|----------------|-------------------------------|
| Copyright | District Court | Magistrate Court (under §63) | Copyright Board |
| Patent | High Court | — | Patent Office opposition |
| Trademark | District Court | Magistrate Court | INDRP / WIPO UDRP |
| Domain Name | — | — | NIXI INDRP / WIPO Arbitration |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Flow for Electronic Evidence Admissibility

### Step 1 — Identify the Digital Evidence
The investigator must first **identify** what the evidence is:
- Email printout? → Produced by a mail server computer.
- CCTV footage? → Produced by a DVR/NVR computer.
- Call Detail Record (CDR)? → Produced by telecom switch computer.

### Step 2 — Verify the Four Conditions of Section 65B(2)

The legal test is structured as a **conjunctive (AND) condition** — all four must be true for admissibility:

$$A_{\text{admissible}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{4} C_i$$

Where:
- $A_{\text{admissible}}$ = Boolean outcome: TRUE only if all 4 conditions are satisfied.
- $C_1$ = Computer used regularly for lawful activity.
- $C_2$ = Information fed in ordinary course.
- $C_3$ = Computer operating properly (or malfunction did not affect record integrity).
- $C_4$ = Record reproduces/derives from fed information.

### Step 3 — Secure the 65B(4) Certificate

The certificate must contain:
1. Identification of the electronic record.
2. The manner in which it was produced.
3. The particulars of the device involved.
4. The conditions of Section 65B(2) certified as true.
5. Signature of the **Certifying Officer**.

$$\text{Certificate Authenticity} = \text{Valid Signatory} \times \text{Accurate Content} \times \text{Timely Issuance}$$

### Step 4 — Maintain Chain of Custody

Forensic chain of custody ensures the evidence has not been tampered with:

$$\text{Integrity} = H_{\text{hash}}(\text{Original Image}) \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{hash}}(\text{Current Image})$$

Where $H_{\text{hash}}$ is typically SHA-256. Any mismatch indicates tampering.

### Step 5 — Apply Relevant Case Law

| Case | Citation | Principle Established |
|------|----------|------------------------|
| *Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer* | (2014) 10 SCC 1 | Section 65B certificate mandatory for electronic evidence |
| *Arjun Panditrao v. Kailash Kushanrao* | (2020) 3 SCC 53 | Certificate required *at the time of production*, not later |
| *State v. Mohd. Afzal* | (2003) Delhi HC | CDR admissible as electronic record under §65B |
| *Ravi Chandran v. State* | (2012) Madras HC | Printout without certificate = inadmissible |
| *Tapan Kumar Singh v. State* | (2017) Delhi HC | Hash value comparison accepted as authenticity proof |

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Note for Examinations:** When answering an evidence question, never say "electronic evidence is admissible." Always say "electronic evidence is admissible **subject to satisfaction of Section 65B conditions and production of the certificate**." The word *subject to* is what examiners award marks for.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Evidence Analysis

**Scenario:** A software company sues a former employee for stealing proprietary source code via email before resignation.

**Step 1 — Identify the IP infringed:** Trade Secret (proprietary source code) + Copyright (the code itself).

**Step 2 — Identify the digital evidence:**
- Email log from the company mail server.
- Forensic image of the employee's laptop.
- USB transfer logs.

**Step 3 — Apply Section 65B(2) tests:**

$$C_1: \text{Mail server used regularly for company email activity} \rightarrow \text{TRUE}$$

$$C_2: \text{Emails sent/received by employee in ordinary course} \rightarrow \text{TRUE}$$

$$C_3: \text{Server operating properly during relevant period} \rightarrow \text{TRUE (verified via server logs)}$$

$$C_4: \text{Printout reproduces the email content as fed into server} \rightarrow \text{TRUE}$$

$$\therefore \bigwedge_{i=1}^{4} C_i = \text{TRUE} \rightarrow \text{Admissible}$$

**Step 4 — Certificate:** IT Manager (responsible official) signs Section 65B(4) certificate authenticating the mail server output.

**Step 5 — Chain of Custody:** Forensic image of laptop hashed using SHA-256; hash value preserved on a write-once medium (CD-R) and produced in court.

**Step 6 — Cause of Action:**
- Civil: Copyright infringement (Copyright Act §55) + breach of confidentiality (Contract Act).
- Criminal: IT Act §66 (computer-related offence) + Copyright Act §63.

**Outcome:** Court admits both the email log and laptop image as evidence; injunction granted; damages awarded.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define Intellectual Property. List any four types of IP relevant to cyber law. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

Intellectual Property (IP) refers to the legal rights that vest in creations of the human mind, granting creators exclusive control over their intangible works for a defined period.

Four types of IP relevant to cyber law:

1. **Copyright** — Protects original software code, digital content, and databases (Copyright Act, 1957).
2. **Patent** — Protects novel inventions including software with technical effect (Patents Act, 1970).
3. **Trademark** — Protects brand names, logos, and domain names (Trade Marks Act, 1999).
4. **Trade Secret** — Protects confidential business information like algorithms and source code (enforced via Contract Law + IT Act).

**Marks Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark; Four types = 0.5 marks each × 4 = 2 marks.

---

### Question 2
**Q: What is a Section 65B certificate? Why is it important for electronic evidence? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Section 65B(4) certificate** is a written declaration, signed by a person occupying a responsible official position in relation to the operation of the device that produced the electronic record, certifying that the four conditions of Section 65B(2) of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 are satisfied.

**Importance:**
1. It is **mandatory** under the Supreme Court's ruling in *Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014)* for electronic evidence to be admissible.
2. Without it, even genuine digital evidence is treated as inadmissible hearsay.
3. It assures the court of the **integrity, authenticity, and reliability** of the electronic record.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition = 1.5 marks; Importance = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain in detail the various forms of Intellectual Property protection applicable to software and digital content under Indian cyber law. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Copyright Protection for Software (3 marks):**
- Under **Section 2(o)** of the Copyright Act, 1957, "literary work" includes computer programs. Hence, both source code and object code enjoy copyright as literary works.
- Protection is **automatic** upon creation and fixation — no registration mandatory (though registration creates a presumption).
- Copyright duration: Lifetime of the author + **60 years**.
- Remedies for infringement: Civil (injunction, damages, account of profits) under §55–§62; Criminal (imprisonment 6 months–3 years + fine ₹50,000–₹2 lakh) under §63.
- Exemptions under **Section 52**: Fair use for research, education, criticism, news reporting, judicial use.

**2. Patent Protection for Software (2 marks):**
- Under **Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970**, "computer programs per se" are excluded.
- However, if the software produces a **technical effect** or is integrated with hardware (e.g., a novel compression algorithm implemented in firmware), it can be patented.
- Duration: **20 years** from filing date.

**3. Trade Secret Protection (1 mark):**
- Source code is often protected as a trade secret through NDAs and confidentiality clauses.
- Remedies under the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and IT Act §66 (computer-related offences).
- Indefinite protection as long as secrecy is maintained.

**4. Design Protection (1 mark):**
- The **Designs Act, 2000** protects the visual/aesthetic appearance of icons, GUIs, and layouts for up to 10 years (renewable for 5 more).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the legal remedies available against software piracy and unauthorized distribution of digital content under the Copyright Act and the IT Act. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**A. Civil Remedies under Copyright Act (3 marks):**
1. **Injunction** (§55) — Temporary or permanent to restrain infringement.
2. **Damages** (§55) — Actual + additional damages for flagrant infringement.
3. **Account of Profits** (§58) — Infringer must surrender profits earned.
4. **Anton Piller Order** (UK origin, applied in India) — Court order allowing the plaintiff to enter defendant's premises to seize infringing material.

**B. Criminal Remedies under Copyright Act (2 marks):**
1. **Section 63** — Imprisonment 6 months to 3 years + fine ₹50,000 to ₹2 lakh.
2. **Section 65A** — Circumventing TPM (DRM) — 2 years imprisonment + fine.
3. **Section 65B** — Removing/altering CMI (Copyright Management Information) — 2 years imprisonment + fine.
4. **Section 66** — Criminal liability for knowingly using infringing copies.

**C. Remedies under IT Act, 2000 (2 marks):**
1. **Section 65** — Tampering with computer source documents — 3 years imprisonment + fine up to ₹2 lakh.
2. **Section 66** — Computer-related offences (hacking, unauthorized access) — 3 years imprisonment + fine up to ₹5 lakh.
3. **Section 67** — Publishing obscene/harmful information — 5 years imprisonment.
4. **Section 69** — Government's power to intercept, monitor, decrypt — relevant for DRM enforcement.

**D. International Cooperation (Marks awarded as enrichment):**
- WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT), 1996.
- TRIPS Agreement, 1994.
- Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: What is electronic evidence? Explain the conditions under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act for admissibility of electronic records. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Electronic evidence is any information of probative value, stored or transmitted in digital form, that is produced in court to prove or disprove a fact.

**Historical Context (1 mark):** Before the IT Act 2000 amendment, electronic records were treated as hearsay under the Indian Evidence Act, 1872. Sections 65A, 65B, 67, and 134 were inserted to make digital evidence admissible.

**The Four Conditions of Section 65B(2) — (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Regular Use Condition:** The computer producing the record was used regularly to store or process information for lawful activity. This ensures reliability and routine operation.

2. **Ordinary Course Input Condition:** The information was fed into the computer in the ordinary course of activities of the person in lawful custody. This prevents post-hoc fabrication.

3. **Proper Operation Condition:** The computer was operating properly or, if not, any malfunction did not affect the accuracy or integrity of the record. Verified through system logs.

4. **Accurate Reproduction Condition:** The electronic record accurately reproduces or derives from the information fed into the computer. This ensures the printout/copy mirrors the original.

**Practical Example (1 mark):** A call detail record (CDR) from a telecom service provider is admissible if the telecom's billing computer was operating regularly, in ordinary course, without malfunction, and the printout reflects the actual call data.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Examine the role of Section 65B(4) certificate in the admissibility of electronic records. Refer to relevant case law. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**A. The Certificate Requirement (3 marks):**
Section 65B(4) mandates that any electronic record produced as evidence must be accompanied by a certificate stating:
- The identity of the electronic record.
- The manner and circumstances of its production.
- The particulars of the device involved.
- The conditions of Section 65B(2) are satisfied.
- The signature and designation of the certifying officer.

**The certifying officer must be**:
- A person occupying a **responsible official position** in relation to the operation of the device.
- Examples: System Administrator, IT Manager, Forensic Analyst, Database Administrator.
- **NOT** the investigating police officer (a common misconception).

**B. Landmark Case Law (3 marks):**

1. **Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014) 10 SCC 1:**
   - Held that the certificate is **mandatory**.
   - Electronic evidence without Section 65B certificate is inadmissible as secondary evidence.
   - Overturned the relaxed approach previously followed by some High Courts.

2. **Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gaud (2020) 3 SCC 53:**
   - Clarified that the certificate must be produced **at the time of evidence production**.
   - Deficiencies cannot be cured later.
   - Retrospective or supplementary certificates are generally not allowed.

3. **State (NCT of Delhi) v. Navjot Sandhu (2005) 10 SCC 600 (Afzal Case):**
   - Earlier relaxed approach where Supreme Court admitted electronic evidence without strict certificate.
   - Now superseded by Anvar P.V.

**C. Practical Implication (1 mark):**
Failure to produce the certificate can result in:
- Entire prosecution evidence being thrown out.
- Acquittal of the accused despite strong circumstantial evidence.
- Hence, digital forensic experts must always preserve and produce the certificate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Certifying Officer with Investigating Officer (–2 marks):** Many students wrongly state that "the police officer investigating the case" issues the 65B certificate. **Correct:** The person in lawful operation of the device (system admin, IT head, forensic analyst).
>
> 2. **Writing "Copyright" instead of "the Copyright Act, 1957" (–1 mark):** Always cite the **Act name and year**, and the specific **section number**. Bare statements without statutory reference lose marks.
>
> 3. **Omitting Section 3(k) when discussing software patents (–1 mark):** The KTU syllabus explicitly tests this exclusion. Any answer on software patents must mention Section 3(k) of the Patents Act, 1970.
>
> 4. **Treating Cybersquatting as merely a domain issue (–1 mark):** You must mention **Section 67 of the IT Act + INDRP** (Indian Domain Name Dispute Resolution Policy) and cite the Satyam Infoway case.
>
> 5. **Writing "electronic evidence is admissible" without the conditional phrase (–2 marks):** Always write: "**Electronic evidence is admissible subject to** Section 65B conditions and production of certificate." This shows legal precision.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning Anvar P.V. (2014) and Arjun Panditrao (2020) cases (–2 marks):** These are mandatory Supreme Court precedents in any evidence answer for KTU valuation.
>
> 7. **Confusing "Idea" with "Expression" in copyright (–1 mark):** Always clarify that **only the expression** (code) is protected, not the underlying idea/algorithm.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Intellectual Property Aspects — Quick Revision Checklist

- [ ] IP = Rights over intangible creations of the mind.
- [ ] Software = **Literary work** under Copyright Act §2(o).
- [ ] **Idea–Expression dichotomy** protects code, not logic.
- [ ] Fair use under §52 (research, criticism, education).
- [ ] TPM circumvention is criminal under §65A (2 years).
- [ ] Software patent requires **technical effect** (§3(k) exclusion).
- [ ] **Cybersquatting** = bad faith domain registration.
- [ ] **INDRP** is the Indian domain dispute resolution mechanism.
- [ ] **Satyam Infoway v. Sifynet** — domain names protected as trademarks in India.
- [ ] **Trade secrets** protected via NDAs + IT Act §66.
- [ ] **Designs Act, 2000** protects GUIs and icons (10 years).
- [ ] **Semiconductor IC Layout Design Act, 2004** for chip designs.
- [ ] WIPO Copyright Treaty (1996) governs international copyright in cyberspace.

### Evidence Aspects — Quick Revision Checklist

- [ ] Electronic evidence governed by **Sections 65A, 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (inserted by IT Act 2000).
- [ ] **Section 65B(2)** — Four cumulative (AND) conditions for admissibility.
- [ ] **Section 65B(4)** — Mandatory certificate from responsible official.
- [ ] Certifying officer = System Admin / IT Manager / Forensic Analyst (NOT the IO).
- [ ] **Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014)** — Certificate is mandatory.
- [ ] **Arjun Panditrao v. Kailash Kushanrao (2020)** — Certificate at time of production.
- [ ] **Chain of Custody** must be maintained for evidence integrity.
- [ ] **Hash values (SHA-256)** verify integrity of forensic images.
- [ ] Volatile evidence (RAM, network) is collected first; non-volatile (disk) later.
- [ ] CDR, CCTV, emails, logs = electronic records under Section 65B.
- [ ] Printouts without certificate = inadmissible as per *Ravi Chandran v. State* (2012).
- [ ] **Section 73A** of Evidence Act — admissibility of electronic signatures.

### Key Statutes to Remember at a Glance

| Statute | Year | Purpose |
|---------|------|---------|
| Indian Evidence Act | 1872 (amended 2000) | Admissibility of evidence |
| Indian Contract Act | 1872 | Trade secrets via NDAs |
| Copyright Act | 1957 (amended 2012) | Software & digital content |
| Patents Act | 1970 (amended 2005) | Software patents |
| Trade Marks Act | 1999 | Brand & domain protection |
| Designs Act | 2000 | GUI & icon protection |
| Information Technology Act | 2000 (amended 2008) | Cyber law framework |
| Semiconductor IC Layout Design Act | 2004 | Chip design protection |

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark question on this combined topic, allocate approximately:
> - 3 marks → Definitions and statutory framework
> - 4 marks → Detailed explanation with section numbers
> - 3 marks → Case law citations (at least 2 cases)
> - 2 marks → Practical examples or current relevance
> - 2 marks → Conclusion or future trends (AI, blockchain, cloud)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
