The right to information is **not absolute**. Section 8 of the Act enumerates **11 specific grounds** where public authorities may deny information:

1. Information disclosure would prejudicially affect the **sovereignty and integrity of India**, security of the State, strategic, scientific, or economic interests, relations with foreign States, or incitement to an offence.
2. Information that has been expressly forbidden to be published by any court of law or tribunal, or the disclosure of which may constitute contempt of court.
3. Information, the disclosure of which would cause a breach of **privilege of Parliament or State Legislature**.
4. Information including commercial confidence, trade secrets, or intellectual property, the disclosure of which would harm the competitive position of a third party, unless such third party consents.
5. Information available in **fiduciary relationship** (e.g., between lawyer and client).
6. Information received in confidence from a foreign government.
7. Information, the disclosure of which would endanger the **life or physical safety** of any person or identify the source of information.
8. Information that would **impede the process of investigation** or apprehension or prosecution of offenders.
9. **Cabinet papers** including records of deliberations of the Council of Ministers, Secretaries, and other officers (subject to the decision of the competent authority to make them public).
10. Information that relates to personal information, the disclosure of which has **no relationship to any public activity** or interest, or which would cause unwarranted invasion of privacy unless public interest justifies disclosure.
11. Information that has been made available to a third party under the **Official Secrets Act, 1923**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 20-Year Rule (Section 8(3)):** Information about an occurrence, event, or matter that has taken place twenty years ago is **NOT exempt** under the grounds of cabinet papers, sovereignty, or fiduciary relationship. This is a KTU favourite!

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Time Limits & Penalties

| Parameter | Time Limit | Statutory Provision | KTU Frequency |
|-----------|------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Standard request for information | **30 days** from receipt | Section 7(1) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Request concerning **life and liberty** | **48 hours** | Section 7(1) proviso | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Request to APIO (transferred request) | 5 days to transfer + 30 days from receipt by PIO | Section 5(2) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Rejection of request | Reply within 30 days with reasons | Section 7(7) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| First Appeal (to First Appellate Authority) | Within **30 days** of expiry/rejection | Section 19(1) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Second Appeal (to CIC/SIC) | Within **90 days** of order of First Appellate Authority | Section 19(3) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Penalty for delay | **₹250 per day** (max ₹25,000) | Section 20(1) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Maximum penalty cap | **₹25,000** per case | Section 20(1) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Third-party appeal | **30 days** | Section 19(2) | ⭐⭐ |

## 2.5 Engineering / Governance Utility of RTI

In the context of **PECST419 — Cyber Ethics, Privacy and Legal Issues**, the RTI Act is highly relevant because:

- **Digital RTI Portals:** Many government departments (e.g., Department of Personnel & Training, Ministry of Home Affairs) have launched online RTI portals where applications can be filed electronically.
- **Cybersecurity and Disclosure:** If a data breach occurs in a public sector organization, an RTI application can be filed seeking information about the breach, response, and remediation.
- **E-Governance Transparency:** Projects like *Digital India*, *MyGov*, and *UMANG* operate on the principle of transparency that the RTI Act strengthens.
- **Personal Data Protection (DPDP Act, 2023) Interplay:** Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act regarding personal information intersects with privacy concerns raised by the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure for Filing an RTI Application

Since the RTI Act is procedural, KTU examiners frequently test the **"step-by-step process"**. Below is the exhaustive derivation of the procedure:

### Step 1: Identify the Public Authority

The applicant must first identify the public authority that holds the desired information.

$$\text{Public Authority} = \{\text{Constitutional Bodies}\} \cup \{\text{Statutory Bodies}\} \cup \{\text{Government-Funded NGOs}\}$$

### Step 2: Draft the Application in English/Hindi/Official Language

The application must be in writing (or electronic mode), in English, Hindi, or the official language of the area. The application must contain:

- Name and contact details of the applicant.
- Name of the public authority.
- Specific question(s) or information sought.
- Application fee of ₹10 (or as prescribed).
- BPL certificate (if applicable for fee waiver).

### Step 3: Pay the Prescribed Fee

$$\text{Fee} = \begin{cases} \text{Rs. 10 (or prescribed amount)} & \text{if applicant is NOT BPL} \\ 0 & \text{if applicant is BPL (Below Poverty Line)} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No fee for BPL applicants** is a statutory right under Section 7(5).

### Step 4: Submit the Application to the PIO

The application is submitted to the **Public Information Officer** of the concerned department. In case of multiple public authorities, a copy is sent to the **Assistant Public Information Officer (APIO)** for transfer.

### Step 5: APIO Transfers (if needed) — Section 5(2)

If the application is received by APIO, transfer logic is:

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = 5 \text{ days from receipt}$$

After transfer, the **30-day clock resets** from the date the application is received by the actual PIO.

### Step 6: PIO Disposes the Request — Section 7(1)

The PIO must dispose the request within the statutory limit:

$$T_{\text{disposal}} = \begin{cases} 30 \text{ days} & \text{Standard request} \\ 48 \text{ hours} & \text{Life and liberty matters} \end{cases}$$

### Step 7: PIO Rejects (if applicable) — Section 7(7)

If the PIO decides to reject the request, the order must:
- Be in writing.
- State the reasons for rejection.
- Mention the name and designation of the officer.
- Be communicated to the applicant within 30 days.

### Step 8: First Appeal — Section 19(1)

If rejected or no response, the applicant can file a first appeal:

$$T_{\text{appeal}} = 30 \text{ days from expiry of } T_{\text{disposal}}$$

The First Appellate Authority must dispose the appeal within:

$$T_{\text{first appeal}} = 30 \text{ days from filing}$$

### Step 9: Second Appeal — Section 19(3)

If the first appeal is also unsatisfactory, the second appeal lies to:

$$\text{Second Appeal Authority} = \begin{cases} \text{CIC (Central Information Commission)} & \text{for Central Govt. matters} \\ \text{SIC (State Information Commission)} & \text{for State Govt. matters} \end{cases}$$

$$T_{\text{second appeal filing}} = 90 \text{ days from order of First Appellate Authority}$$

### Step 10: Penalty Imposition — Section 20

The CIC/SIC can impose penalty for delay:

$$P_{\text{penalty}} = \sum_{d=1}^{n} (250 \times d) \quad \text{subject to } P_{\text{penalty}} \leq 25{,}000$$

Where:
- $d$ = number of days of delay
- Maximum cap = ₹25,000 per case

**Worked Example:**

Suppose a PIO delays information on a routine matter by **50 days** (after the 30-day limit). Calculate the penalty.

$$
P_{\text{penalty}} = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500
$$

Since 12,500 < 25,000, the full penalty of **₹12,500** is applicable.

Now, suppose a PIO delays by **120 days**:

$$
P_{\text{calculated}} = 250 \times 120 = 30{,}000
$$

Since 30,000 > 25,000, the cap applies:

$$
P_{\text{actual}} = \min(30{,}000, 25{,}000) = 25{,}000
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Composition of Information Commissions

### 3.2.1 Central Information Commission (CIC) — Section 12

$$\text{CIC Composition} = 1 \text{ Chief Information Commissioner} + \text{up to } 10 \text{ Information Commissioners}$$

- Appointed by: **The President of India** on the recommendation of a committee.
- Term: **5 years** or until attaining **65 years** of age (whichever is earlier).
- Salary: Equivalent to the salary of a **Chief Election Commissioner**.

### 3.2.2 State Information Commission (SIC) — Section 15

$$\text{SIC Composition} = 1 \text{ State Chief Information Commissioner} + \text{up to } 10 \text{ State Information Commissioners}$$

- Appointed by: **The Governor** on the recommendation of a committee.
- Term: **5 years** or until attaining **65 years** of age (whichever is earlier).
- Salary: Equivalent to the salary of an **Election Commissioner** of the State.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Powers of CIC/SIC (Section 18 & 20)

The CIC and SIC have the powers of a **Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, in respect of:

1. Summoning and enforcing the attendance of persons.
2. Requiring the discovery and production of documents.
3. Receiving evidence on affidavits.
4. Requisitioning any public record or copy thereof.
5. Issuing commissions for the examination of witnesses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** "CIC has the powers of a civil court" is a frequently asked 3-mark question. Memorize the 5 powers verbatim.

## 3.4 Penalties and Disciplinary Action — Complete Derivation

### Section 20 Framework

| Action | Trigger | Quantum | Authority |
|--------|---------|---------|-----------|
| Penalty for delay | PIO delays beyond 30 days | ₹250/day, max ₹25,000 | CIC/SIC |
| Penalty for refusal | PIO refuses without reasonable cause | ₹250/day, max ₹25,000 | CIC/SIC |
| Malafide refusal | PIO acts in bad faith | Up to ₹25,000 + recommendation for disciplinary action | CIC/SIC |
| Recommendation for disciplinary action | Persistent non-compliance | Recommendation to competent authority | CIC/SIC |

### Section 20(2) — Exceptions

No penalty shall be imposed if the PIO proves that:
- The delay was for reasons beyond his/her control.
- The PIO acted in good faith.

> [!NOTE]
> **Burden of Proof:** The burden lies on the PIO to prove the exception applies. This is a key principle of the RTI Act's accountability architecture.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Public Authority" under the Right to Information Act, 2005. Mention at least one category of non-government entity that is included within the definition.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Under **Section 2(h)** of the Right to Information Act, 2005, a **Public Authority** means any authority or body or institution of self-government established or constituted:
- By or under the Constitution,
- By any law made by Parliament,
- By any law made by a State Legislature,
- By a notification issued by the appropriate Government.

It also includes any **non-government organization substantially financed, directly or indirectly, by funds provided by the appropriate Government**.

**Example of a non-government entity included:** A privately-managed school receiving more than 50% of its annual expenditure from government grants is treated as a public authority for the purpose of the RTI Act.

*(Examiner's Note: 3 marks — 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the time limit prescribed under the RTI Act for providing information that concerns the "life and liberty" of a person?

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Under **Section 7(1) proviso** of the RTI Act, 2005, where the information sought concerns the **life or liberty of a person**, the Public Information Officer is required to provide the information **within forty-eight (48) hours** of the receipt of the request.

This special provision exists to ensure that in emergencies, citizens are not denied time-critical information that may affect their fundamental right to life and personal liberty under **Article 21** of the Constitution.

*(Examiner's Note: 3 marks — 2 marks for the time limit + 1 mark for the constitutional linkage.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Right to Information Act, 2005 — Comprehensive Analysis

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain in detail the salient features of the Right to Information Act, 2005. Discuss its applicability to non-government organizations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Salient Features of the RTI Act, 2005:**

The RTI Act, 2005 is recognized as one of the most progressive transparency laws in the world. Its salient features are as follows:

**1. Universal Applicability:** The Act applies to the whole of India, covering all constitutional authorities, statutory bodies, public sector undertakings, and government-funded institutions. It binds the Central Government, all State Governments, and local self-government bodies (Panchayats and Municipalities).

**2. Time-Bound Disposal:** Information must be provided within **30 days** of the request. In cases involving life and liberty, the timeline is reduced to **48 hours**.

**3. Fee Waiver for BPL:** Persons below the poverty line (BPL) are exempted from paying any fee.

**4. Independent Oversight:** The Act establishes the Central Information Commission (CIC) and State Information Commissions (SICs) as independent bodies to adjudicate appeals and complaints. These bodies enjoy the powers of a civil court.

**5. Penalties for Non-Compliance:** PIOs can be penalized up to **₹25,000** for delays or refusals, and disciplinary action can be recommended for persistent non-compliance.

**6. Two-Tier Appeal:** A citizen unsatisfied with the PIO's response can approach the First Appellate Authority, and subsequently, the CIC or SIC.

**7. Mandatory Proactive Disclosure:** Section 4 mandates that every public authority suo motu publish 17 categories of information, including organizational structure, budget, subsidies, and information on public procurement.

**Applicability to Non-Government Organizations (NGOs):**

The Act extends its reach to non-government entities under **Section 2(h)** read with **Section 2(2)(i)**, which includes any NGO substantially financed by the appropriate Government. The term "substantially financed" has been judicially interpreted to mean **more than 50% of the organization's expenditure** being met by government grants.

The rationale behind including NGOs is:
- They perform public functions using public money.
- Accountability to the public is essential.
- They are discharging sovereign or statutory functions.

The landmark case of **Sahara India Real Estate Corporation Ltd. v. Securities and Exchange Board of India (2012)** and several CIC decisions have clarified that even private entities discharging public duties may be brought under the RTI framework.

---

**(b) Describe the composition, powers, and functions of the Central Information Commission. What is the penalty that can be imposed on a Public Information Officer for non-compliance? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Composition of the Central Information Commission (Section 12):**

- The CIC consists of a **Chief Information Commissioner** and **up to ten Information Commissioners**.
- They are appointed by the **President of India** on the recommendation of a committee comprising:
  - The Prime Minister (Chairperson),
  - The Leader of Opposition in the Lok Sabha,
  - A Union Cabinet Minister nominated by the Prime Minister.
- **Term of Office:** 5 years from the date of entering office, OR until attaining the age of **65 years**, whichever is earlier.
- **Salary:** Equivalent to the salary of the **Chief Election Commissioner**.
- They are **not eligible for reappointment**.

**Powers of the CIC (Section 18):**

The CIC has the powers of a **Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, specifically:
1. Summoning and enforcing the attendance of persons.
2. Requiring discovery and production of documents.
3. Receiving evidence on affidavits.
4. Requisitioning any public record or copy thereof.
5. Issuing commissions for the examination of witnesses.

**Functions of the CIC:**
- Adjudicate second appeals and complaints.
- Direct PIOs to provide information.
- Impose penalties on defaulting PIOs.
- Recommend disciplinary action against PIOs.
- Submit annual reports to the Central Government on the implementation of the Act.

**Penalty on Public Information Officer (Section 20):**

- **Quantum:** ₹250 per day of delay, subject to a maximum of **₹25,000**.
- **Trigger:** Delay in providing information or refusal to provide information without reasonable cause.
- **Burden of Proof:** The PIO must prove that the delay was beyond their control or that they acted in good faith.
- **Disciplinary Action:** If the CIC is satisfied that the PIO has acted malafide, it can recommend disciplinary action to the competent authority.

---

### Question B: RTI Act, 2005 — Exemptions, Procedure, and Penalties

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

**(a) List and explain any seven grounds of exemption from disclosure of information under Section 8 of the RTI Act, 2005. What is the 20-year rule? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Section 8(1)** of the RTI Act, 2005 enumerates **11 grounds of exemption** where public authorities may refuse to disclose information. Seven key grounds are explained below:

**1. Sovereignty, Security, and Strategic Interests (Section 8(1)(a)):**
Information that would prejudicially affect the sovereignty and integrity of India, the security of the State, strategic, scientific, or economic interests, relations with foreign States, or lead to incitement of an offence.

**2. Court Orders and Contempt (Section 8(1)(b)):**
Information expressly forbidden to be published by a court of law or tribunal, or the disclosure of which may constitute contempt of court.

**3. Parliamentary Privilege (Section 8(1)(c)):**
Information that would cause a breach of privilege of Parliament or a State Legislature.

**4. Commercial Confidence and Intellectual Property (Section 8(1)(d)):**
Information including commercial confidence, trade secrets, or intellectual property, the disclosure of which would harm the competitive position of a third party, unless that party consents.

**5. Fiduciary Relationship (Section 8(1)(e)):**
Information available in a fiduciary relationship, where the person receiving the information is in a position of trust and confidence.

**6. Foreign Government Information (Section 8(1)(f)):**
Information received in confidence from a foreign government.

**7. Life and Physical Safety (Section 8(1)(g)):**
Information, the disclosure of which would endanger the life or physical safety of any person or identify the source of information.

**8. Cabinet Papers (Section 8(1)(i)):**
Information relating to cabinet papers, including records of deliberations of the Council of Ministers, Secretaries, and other officers. Disclosure of cabinet papers is at the discretion of the competent authority.

**9. Personal Information (Section 8(1)(j)):**
Information that relates to personal information, the disclosure of which has no relationship to any public activity, or which would cause unwarranted invasion of privacy. However, this exemption can be overridden if the public interest in disclosure outweighs the privacy concern.

**10. Official Secrets Act, 1923 (Section 8(1)(k)):**
Information that has been made available to a third party under the Official Secrets Act, 1923.

**The 20-Year Rule (Section 8(3)):**

Under **Section 8(3)** of the Act, the grounds of exemption related to **sovereignty, cabinet papers, and fiduciary relationships shall not apply** to information about an occurrence, event, or matter which has taken place **twenty years or more** before the date of the request.

This provision is a crucial safeguard against the permanent classification of historical records. It ensures that:
- Government records are eventually accessible to citizens.
- Historical accountability is preserved.
- Government is not allowed to indefinitely hide information under vague grounds.

For example, a citizen cannot request top-secret WWII records, but can request files from 2004 or earlier (assuming the current year is 2024) that may have been previously classified as cabinet papers.

---

**(b) Explain the step-by-step procedure for filing an RTI application. What are the time limits and fees prescribed? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-Step Procedure for Filing an RTI Application:**

**Step 1: Identify the Public Authority**
The applicant must first identify the public authority that holds the information. Public authorities include all government departments, constitutional bodies, statutory corporations, and substantially-funded NGOs.

**Step 2: Draft the Application**
The application must be in writing (or electronic mode), in English, Hindi, or the official language of the State/UT. It should contain:
- Applicant's name and contact details.
- Name of the public authority.
- Specific information sought.
- Application fee (₹10 or as prescribed).
- BPL certificate (if applicable).

**Step 3: Payment of Fee**
- Standard fee: **₹10** for Central Government (or as prescribed by the State).
- BPL applicants: **No fee**.
- Mode of payment: Cash, Demand Draft, Postal Order, Court Fee Stamp, or electronic payment (where online portals are available).

**Step 4: Submission to PIO or APIO**
The application is submitted to the **Public Information Officer (PIO)**. If sent to the **Assistant Public Information Officer (APIO)** in larger departments, the APIO must transfer it to the concerned PIO within **5 days**.

**Step 5: Disposal by PIO**
The PIO must dispose of the application:
- **Within 30 days** of receipt (standard case).
- **Within 48 hours** if the request concerns life and liberty.
- The 30-day clock starts from the date the PIO receives the application.

**Step 6: Rejection (if applicable)**
If the PIO decides to reject, the order must be in writing with reasons, mention the grounds of exemption, and be communicated within 30 days.

**Step 7: First Appeal — Section 19(1)**
If the applicant is unsatisfied or receives no response within 30 days, a first appeal lies to the **First Appellate Authority** (an officer senior in rank to the PIO).
- Time limit for filing: **30 days** from the expiry of 30 days or from the date of rejection.
- Time limit for disposal: **30 days** from filing.

**Step 8: Second Appeal — Section 19(3)**
If unsatisfied with the first appellate order, the second appeal lies to the **Central Information Commission** (for Central Government matters) or **State Information Commission** (for State Government matters).
- Time limit for filing: **90 days** from the date of the first appellate order.
- The CIC/SIC has the powers of a civil court.

**Step 9: Penalty**
The CIC/SIC can impose a penalty of **₹250 per day** of delay on the PIO, subject to a maximum of **₹25,000**.

**Fees Summary Table:**

| Component | Quantum |
|-----------|---------|
| Application fee | ₹10 (Central Govt.) |
| BPL applicants | Nil |
| Additional fee (cost of providing information) | Reasonable charges |
| Time limit for standard request | 30 days |
| Time limit for life/liberty | 48 hours |
| First appeal filing | 30 days |
| Second appeal filing | 90 days |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse the 30-day limit for first appeal filing. The 30-day limit is from the **expiry of the PIO's 30-day period** (i.e., 60 days total from original application), not from the date of application. Writing this incorrectly can cost 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Students write "₹10" as the application fee for all states. The fee for Central Government is ₹10, but each state has its own prescribed fee. Always specify "for Central Government" or check state rules.
>
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the **20-year rule under Section 8(3)**. This is a KTU favourite. If a question asks about cabinet papers, always mention the 20-year rule.
>
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the appointment authority of CIC (President) with SIC (Governor). This is a common 1-mark loss.
>
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** The CIC has civil court powers, NOT criminal court powers. Do not write "all powers of a court".
>
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** In the penalty calculation, students often forget the **maximum cap of ₹25,000**. Always apply $\min(\text{calculated}, 25{,}000)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Enactment Date:** 15 June 2005; **Enforcement Date:** 12 October 2005 (which happens to be **Mahatma Gandhi's birth anniversary**, deliberately chosen).
- **Original Scope:** Did not apply to Jammu & Kashmir (post-2019 abrogation, now applies).
- **Number of Sections:** 31; **Chapters:** 7; **Schedules:** 2.
- **Key Definitions:** Information, Right to Information, Public Authority, PIO, APIO, CPIO, Central/State Information Commission.
- **Public Authority includes:** Constitutional bodies, statutory bodies, and substantially-financed NGOs.
- **Section 4:** Mandatory **proactive disclosure** of 17 categories of information by every public authority.
- **Section 6:** Right to request information by any citizen (no need to disclose purpose).
- **Section 7(1):** 30-day standard limit; **48 hours for life and liberty**.
- **Section 7(5):** BPL applicants are exempt from all fees.
- **Section 7(7):** Rejection must be in writing with reasons and mention exemption ground.
- **Section 8(1):** 11 grounds of exemption.
- **Section 8(3):** The **20-year rule** — information older than 20 years is NOT exempt on grounds of sovereignty, cabinet papers, or fiduciary relationship.
- **Section 8(1)(j):** Personal information exemption; can be overridden by larger public interest.
- **Section 9:** Grounds for rejection must be severable — provide partial information where possible.
- **Section 10:** Third-party information requires notice and representation.
- **Section 11:** Exempted information can be disclosed if larger public interest justifies it.
- **Section 12:** Composition of CIC — 1 Chief + up to 10 Information Commissioners, appointed by **President**.
- **Section 13, 14:** Term of office = 5 years OR age 65, whichever is earlier.
- **Section 15:** Composition of SIC — 1 State Chief + up to 10 Information Commissioners, appointed by **Governor**.
- **Section 18:** CIC/SIC has powers of a **Civil Court** (5 specific powers).
- **Section 19(1):** First appeal to First Appellate Authority within **30 days** of expiry/rejection.
- **Section 19(2):** Third-party appeal within **30 days**.
- **Section 19(3):** Second appeal to CIC/SIC within **90 days** of first appellate order.
- **Section 20:** Penalty = **₹250/day, max ₹25,000**; burden of proof on PIO to prove good faith.
- **Section 21:** CIC/SIC order is final, but **judicial review** lies to the High Court under Article 226/227.
- **Section 22:** Act has **overriding effect** over the Official Secrets Act, 1923.
- **Section 23:** No suit, prosecution, or other legal proceeding shall lie against any officer for anything done in good faith.
- **Section 24:** Intelligence and security organizations (RAW, IB, CBI, etc.) are **exempted** from the Act, but information relating to corruption and human rights violations must be disclosed.
- **Section 25:** Protection of action taken in good faith.
- **First Appellate Authority** must be an officer senior in rank to the PIO.
- **Salary of CIC** = Chief Election Commissioner; **Salary of State CIC** = State Election Commissioner.
- **Committees for Appointment:** PM-led committee for CIC; CM-led committee for SIC.
- **RTI is not absolute** — exemptions are codified under Section 8, and **public interest test** under Section 11 can override certain exemptions.
- **Right to Information is a derivative of Article 19(1)(a)** (Freedom of Speech and Expression) and **Article 21** (Right to Life and Liberty).
- **Cybersecurity Implication:** Online RTI portals must comply with RTI timelines and Information Technology Act, 2000 for digital records.
- **Interplay with DPDP Act 2023:** Section 8(1)(j) personal information exemption aligns with the data minimization principle of DPDP Act.
- **Filing fee:** ₹10 (Central) — varies by state.
- **No RTI fees for BPL applicants.**
- **No requirement to disclose reasons** for seeking information (Section 6(2)).
- **Annual Reports:** CIC submits to Central Government; SICs to State Government (Section 27).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For PECST419, the RTI Act is most often tested through:
> 1. Definitions of key terms (3 marks).
> 2. Time limits and penalty calculations (3 marks).
> 3. Exemptions under Section 8 + 20-year rule (7 marks).
> 4. Step-by-step procedure for filing RTI (7 marks).
> 5. Composition and powers of CIC/SIC (7 marks).
>
> Practice a 14-mark answer that combines the procedure, time limits, and penalty calculation for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IT Act, 2008 and Its Amendments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST419 - CYBER ETHICS, PRIVACY AND LEGAL ISSUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IT Act, 2008 and Its Amendments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Information Technology Act, 2000** (originally enacted on 9 June 2000) is the primary cyber law of India that provides legal recognition to **electronic commerce**, **electronic records**, **digital signatures**, and aims to facilitate the filing of documents with government agencies in electronic form. The **Information Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008** (notified on 27 October 2009) is a major overhaul that expanded the scope of the parent Act to address contemporary cyber threats, redefined offences, introduced new categories of computer-related crimes, established the **Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In)** as a statutory body, and added provisions on data protection, privacy, and interception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The IT Act 2008 (as amended) is the foundational legal instrument governing cyberspace activities in India. Students must remember that "**IT Act, 2008**" colloquially refers to the **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008**, which amends the original **IT Act, 2000**. The parent Act remains the IT Act, 2000.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cyber Traffic Law"

Think of the IT Act, 2008 as the **"Highway Code of the Internet"** for India.

- The **original IT Act, 2000** was like an early road safety law that addressed only basic things: a few speed limits (digital signatures), basic licensing (electronic records), and minor traffic rules.
- The **2008 Amendment** is like a major upgrade — adding lanes for e-commerce, installing CCTV cameras (interception), creating new rules for autonomous vehicles (cloud computing, biometrics), and introducing severe penalties for hit-and-run cyber crimes.
- **CERT-In** plays the role of the **Highway Patrol / Traffic Police** — the dedicated agency that monitors the "cyber highway," responds to accidents (cyber incidents), and issues safety advisories.
- The **Adjudicating Officer** is the **Sub-Magistrate** who handles minor offences; the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CyAT)** is the **High Court** for cyber appeals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Body Mentioned in the Act:**
> - **CERT-In** — Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (Section 70B)
> - **CyAT** — Cyber Appellate Tribunal (Section 48–64 of original Act, now transferred to **TDSAT** under the **Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021**)
> - **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** — Section 17–34 (now under **CCA, MeitY**)

### 1.3 Core Terminology with Standard Metrics

| Term | Bold Definition | Statutory Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Signature** | Authentication of any electronic record by a subscriber through a cryptographic method | Section 2(1)(p) |
| **Electronic Record** | Data, record or data generated, image or sound stored, received or sent in electronic form | Section 2(1)(t) |
| **Computer** | Any electronic, magnetic, optical or other high-speed data processing device | Section 2(1)(i) |
| **Intermediary** | Any person who on behalf of another receives, stores or transmits electronic records | Section 2(1)(w) |
| **Cyber Café** | Any establishment from where access to the internet is offered for use to members of the public | Section 2(1)(na) |
| **Computer Resource** | Computer, computer system, computer network, data, software, or database | Section 2(1)(k) |
| **Certifying Authority (CA)** | A person who has been granted a licence to issue Digital Signature Certificates | Section 2(1)(g) |
| **Critical Information Infrastructure (CII)** | Computer resource whose destruction would have debilitating impact on national security, economy, public health | Section 2(1)(j) |
| **Penalty** | **Monetary compensation** (not a criminal fine) imposed for breach of statutory duty — up to **₹5 crore** in some cases | Section 43A |
| **Offence** | A criminal act punishable with imprisonment and/or fine | Chapter XI (Sections 65–78) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of the IT Act, 2000 (as amended in 2008)

The Act is organized into **13 Chapters** containing **94 Sections** (after 2008 amendment) and **4 Schedules**. The architecture is layered as follows:

**Layer 1 — Authentication & Legal Recognition (Chapters II–IV)**
- Chapter II: Digital Signature & Electronic Records
- Chapter III: Electronic Governance
- Chapter IV: Regulation of Certifying Authorities (Sections 17–34)

**Layer 2 — Penalties & Compensation (Chapters IX–X)**
- Chapter IX: Penalties, Compensation and Adjudication (Sections 43–47)
- Chapter X: The Cyber Appellate Tribunal (Sections 48–64)

**Layer 3 — Offences & Penalties (Chapter XI)**
- Sections 65–78 — all criminal offences

**Layer 4 — Regulatory & Miscellaneous (Chapters XII–XIII)**
- Section 69–69B: Interception, Monitoring, Blocking
- Section 70: Protected Systems
- Section 70A–70B: NCIIPC and CERT-In
- Section 79: Intermediary Liability Safe Harbour
- Section 81: Act to have overriding effect

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown — Why 2008 Amendment Was Required

1. **Rapid Tech Evolution (2000–2008):** The original 2000 Act addressed only digital signatures and e-commerce, ignoring newer threats like phishing, identity theft, data leakage, and VoIP misuse.
2. **UNCITRAL Model Law Alignment:** India wanted to align with the **UNCITRAL Model Law on Electronic Signatures (2001)** — hence the 2008 Amendment de-emphasized "digital signature" and recognized **"electronic signature"** as a broader concept.
3. **Budapest Convention Pressure:** India needed to criminalize acts consistent with the **Council of Europe's Cybercrime Convention (2001)**, though India is not a signatory.
4. **Terror & National Security Concerns:** Post-26/11 Mumbai attacks, the need for **real-time interception** powers became paramount.
5. **E-commerce Boom:** Definition of e-commerce, consumer protection, and data breach liability required statutory backing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Stat:** The 2008 Amendment inserted **12 new sections** (43A, 48–50 modifications, 66A–66F, 67A–67C, 69A, 69B, 70A, 70B, 79, 81, etc.) and modified **23 existing sections**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Sections, Penalties & Punishment Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` notation in tables. The following is the consolidated "exam-ready" reference matrix.**

| Section | Offence / Provision | Punishment / Penalty | KTU Priority |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **43** | Damage to computer/computer system | Civil compensation up to **₹1 crore** | High |
| **43A** | Failure to protect data (body corporate) | Civil compensation up to **₹5 crore** | **Very High** |
| **44** | Failure to furnish information / returns | Fine up to **₹1.5 lakh** per day | Medium |
| **45** | Residuary penalty | Compensation not exceeding **₹25,000** | Low |
| **47** | Compensation for failure to protect data | Adjudication by officer not below **₹7 lakh** rank | Medium |
| **65** | Tampering with computer source documents | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹2 lakh** or both | **Very High** |
| **66** | Computer-related offences (hacking + mischief) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹5 lakh** or both | **Very High** |
| **66A** ⚠️ | Sending offensive messages | **STRUCK DOWN** in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India* (2015) | **Very High** |
| **66B** | Dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh** | High |
| **66C** | Identity theft (using password/electronic signature of another) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | **Very High** |
| **66D** | Cheating by personation using computer resource | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | High |
| **66E** | Violation of privacy (capturing/publishing images of private parts) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to **₹2 lakh** or both | High |
| **66F** | Cyber terrorism | Imprisonment up to **life imprisonment** | **Very High** |
| **67** | Publishing obscene material in electronic form | 1st offence: up to **5 years** + ₹1 lakh; 2nd: up to **7 years** + ₹2 lakh | **Very High** |
| **67A** | Publishing sexually explicit material | 1st: up to **5 years** + ₹10 lakh; 2nd: up to **7 years** + ₹20 lakh | High |
| **67B** | Child pornography | 1st: up to **5 years** + ₹10 lakh; subsequent: up to **7 years** + ₹20 lakh | High |
| **67C** | Failure to maintain records (intermediary) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine | Medium |
| **68** | Failure/refusal to comply with directions | Imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh/day** | Medium |
| **69** | Interception / monitoring / decryption of info | Imprisonment up to **7 years** + fine | High |
| **69A** | Blocking of websites in public interest | As per IT (Procedure & Safeguards for Blocking for Access of Information by Public) Rules, 2009 | High |
| **69B** | Collection of traffic data / metadata | Authorized agencies only | Medium |
| **70** | Unauthorised access to protected systems | Imprisonment up to **10 years** + fine | **Very High** |
| **70A** | National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (**NCIIPC**) | Designated as Nodal Agency | High |
| **70B** | **CERT-In** — Indian Computer Emergency Response Team | Statutory status; designated as national agency for incident response | **Very High** |
| **71** | Misrepresentation to Controller of CA | Imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Low |
| **72** | Breach of confidentiality / privacy by intermediary | Imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | High |
| **72A** | Disclosure of information in breach of contract | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹5 lakh** | High |
| **73** | Publishing Digital Signature Certificate false in particulars | Imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Low |
| **74** | Creating/Publishing false Digital Signature Certificate | Imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh** | Low |
| **75** | Act applies to offences outside India | Extraterritorial jurisdiction (any offence by any person anywhere) | High |
| **76** | Confiscation of computer resource, etc. | Court-ordered confiscation | Low |
| **77** | Compensation, Penalties or damages NOT bars criminal prosecution | Concurrent liability | Medium |
| **78** | Power to investigate offences | Inspector level or above police officer | Medium |
| **79** | Intermediary safe harbour | Exempt from liability if **due diligence** followed | **Very High** |
| **79A** | Buffer/hosting liability for intermediaries | Exemption conditions | High |
| **80** | Power of police officer to enter, search, etc. | With warrant or in prescribed conditions | Medium |
| **81** | Act to have overriding effect | Overrides other laws in case of conflict | High |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **practical industry contexts**, the IT Act, 2008 is directly relevant to:

- **Software Engineers**: Building e-commerce platforms that need Section 79 intermediary compliance.
- **Data Engineers / DBAs**: Implementing reasonable security practices under **Section 43A** and **SPDI Rules, 2011**.
- **Network Engineers**: Designing systems that can support lawful interception requests under **Section 69**.
- **Security Analysts (SOC/CSIRT)**: Coordinating with **CERT-In** during incidents under **Section 70B(6)**.
- **AI/ML Engineers**: Note **IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021** applicable to AI intermediaries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis — Classification of Penalties vs. Offences

The 2008 Amendment clarified a critical legal distinction that is **frequently asked in KTU exams**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penalty ≠ Offence**
> - A **Penalty** is a **civil** monetary compensation — decided by the **Adjudicating Officer** (not a criminal court).
> - An **Offence** is a **criminal** act — decided by a **Magistrate / Court**, punishable with imprisonment + fine.

**Logical Derivation:**

$$
\text{Penalty} \xrightarrow{\text{Sections 43, 43A, 44, 45, 47}} \text{Adjudicating Officer (Civil remedy)}
$$

$$
\text{Offence} \xrightarrow{\text{Sections 65 to 78}} \text{Magistrate/Court (Criminal remedy)}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. **Step 1:** Identify if the act causes harm but is not necessarily criminal intent → Apply Section 43 (civil damage).
2. **Step 2:** If the act is by a body corporate handling sensitive personal data → Apply Section 43A (compensation up to ₹5 crore).
3. **Step 3:** If the act involves criminal intent (mens rea) such as hacking, theft, fraud, obscenity → Apply Chapter XI (Sections 65–77).
4. **Step 4:** Section 77 explicitly states that paying a penalty does NOT bar criminal prosecution — they are concurrent.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Section 79 (Intermediary Safe Harbour) Logic

The 2008 Amendment introduced Section 79 to protect Internet Service Providers (ISPs), web hosts, search engines, e-commerce platforms, and social media from being held liable for third-party content.

**Conditions for Safe Harbour (Logical Flow):**

$$
\text{Intermediary Liability Shield} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i
$$

where $C_i$ represents the following mandatory conditions:

$$
C_1: \text{Intermediary's role is limited to "providing access" or "transmission" or "hosting"}
$$

$$
C_2: \text{Intermediary did NOT initiate the transmission}
$$

$$
C_3: \text{Intermediary did NOT select the receiver of transmission}
$$

$$
C_4: \text{Intermediary did NOT select/modify the information contained in the transmission}
$$

$$
C_5: \text{Intermediary observes "due diligence" as prescribed under IT (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules, 2011 / 2021}
$$

$$
C_6: \text{Intermediary acts within 72 hours of receiving court order or govt. notification to remove/disable content}
$$

**Conclusion of the derivation:**

If all $C_i$ conditions are satisfied, the intermediary is **exempt from liability** for third-party content. If any one condition fails, the safe harbour is **lost**, and the intermediary becomes liable.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Analysis — The *Shreya Singhal* Judgment and Section 66A

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Important KTU Question Topic**

In **Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)**, the Supreme Court of India **struck down Section 66A** as unconstitutional on the grounds of:

1. **Violation of Article 19(1)(a)** — Freedom of Speech and Expression
2. **Vagueness and Overbreadth** — Terms like "grossly offensive," "menacing," "annoying" were found to be **vague and subjective**
3. **Chilling Effect on Free Speech** — The mere existence of the law had a chilling effect on legitimate online discourse

**Pre vs. Post Shreya Singhal:**

$$
\text{Section 66A (Pre-2015)}: \text{Sending "offensive or menacing" messages} \Rightarrow \text{3 years imprisonment}
$$

$$
\text{Section 66A (Post-2015)}: \text{STRUCK DOWN} \Rightarrow \text{Not enforceable}
$$

**However**, acts that were punishable under Section 66A may now be covered by:
- **Section 505 IPC** (Statements conducing to public mischief)
- **Section 153A IPC** (Promoting enmity between groups)
- **Section 507 IPC** (Criminal intimidation by anonymous communication)
- **Section 124A IPC** (Sedition, though under review)

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis — CERT-In and Section 70B Functions

The 2008 Amendment elevated CERT-In to a **statutory authority**. Its powers and functions are:

$$
\text{CERT-In Functions} = \{F_1, F_2, F_3, F_4, F_5, F_6\}
$$

Where:

- $F_1$: Collect, analyse and disseminate information on cyber incidents
- $F_2$: Forecast and give alerts on cybersecurity incidents
- $F_3$: Take emergency measures for handling cyber security incidents
- $F_4$: Coordinate cyber incident response activities
- $F_5$: Issue guidelines, advisories, vulnerability notes and whitepapers
- $F_6$: Such other functions as may be prescribed

> [!NOTE]
> **CERT-In Directions of 2022:** Under Section 70B(6), CERT-In issued the **Cyber Security Directions of 28 April 2022** which mandated:
> - Synchronization of logs to be maintained for **180 days**
> - Reporting of cyber incidents within **6 hours** of detection
> - KYC and ownership information of subscribers/vendors to be maintained for **5 years**
> - Data centres, VPN providers, and crypto exchanges to register with CERT-In

### 3.5 Comparative Analysis — IT Act 2000 vs. IT (Amendment) Act 2008

| Aspect | IT Act, 2000 | IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | E-commerce, digital signatures | Cyber crimes, data protection, national security |
| Digital Signature | Cryptographic only | Broader "Electronic Signature" concept |
| Cyber Terrorism | Not defined | Defined under Section 66F (Life imprisonment) |
| Child Pornography | Not specifically addressed | Section 67B (specific offence) |
| Privacy | Not addressed | Section 66E (Violation of privacy) |
| CERT-In | Did not exist | Statutory body under Section 70B |
| Intermediary Liability | Not addressed | Safe harbour under Section 79 |
| Data Protection | Not addressed | Section 43A + SPDI Rules 2011 |
| Offences (Count) | ~10 sections | **24 criminal offences** |
| Extraterritorial Jurisdiction | Limited | Section 75 — Universal jurisdiction |
| Penalties Cap | ₹1 crore | ₹5 crore (Section 43A) |
| Critical Information Infra | Not defined | Section 70 + 70A (NCIIPC) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Structural Architecture of the IT Act, 2008 (as amended)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IT Act, 2000 + Amendment 2008] --> B[Chapters II - IV]
    A --> C[Chapters IX - X]
    A --> D[Chapter XI]
    A --> E[Chapters XII - XIII]

    B --> B1[Digital Signature]
    B --> B2[Electronic Governance]
    B --> B3[Certifying Authority]

    C --> C1[Penalties Sections 43-47]
    C --> C2[Adjudicating Officer]
    C --> C3[Cyber Appellate Tribunal CyAT]

    D --> D1[Sections 65-70: Critical Offences]
    D --> D2[Sections 71-77: Other Offences]

    E --> E1[Interception Section 69]
    E --> E2[Protected Systems Section 70]
    E --> E3[CERT-In Section 70B]
    E --> E4[Intermediary Safe Harbour Section 79]
    E --> E5[Overriding Effect Section 81]

    B3 --> F1[Controller of CA - MeitY]
    C2 --> F2[Adjudication under IT Act]
    C3 --> F3[Appeals - TDSAT since 2021]
    D1 --> F4[Criminal Prosecution - Magistrate]
    E3 --> F5[CERT-In Nodal Agency]
    E4 --> F6[IT Rules 2011 / 2021]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Compliance Flow under Section 79 (Intermediary Due Diligence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Uploads Content] --> B{Intermediary receives notice or court order}
    B --> C[Genuine Court Order under 79 3 a]
    B --> D[Government Agency Order under 79 3 b]
    B --> E[Voluntary User Complaint under 79 3 c]

    C --> F[Act within 36 hours of order]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Action within 15 to 30 days of complaint]

    F --> H{Content illegal or violates law}
    G --> H

    H -->|Yes| I[Remove or Disable Access]
    H -->|No| J[Retain Content]

    I --> K[Maintain Records for 180 days]
    J --> K

    K --> L{Safe Harbour Status}
    L -->|Due Diligence Followed| M[Protected under Section 79]
    L -->|Failure| N[Liability Attaches]

    M --> O[Intermediary Continues Operations]
    N --> P[Legal Proceedings Initiated]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Amendment Timeline of the IT Act

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Year 2000 - IT Act 2000 Enacted] --> B[Year 2008 - Major Amendment]
    B --> C[2008 Amendments In Force 27 Oct 2009]
    C --> D[2011 - SPDI Rules notified]
    D --> E[2011 - Intermediary Guidelines Rules]
    E --> F[2013 - IT Rules Amended]
    F --> G[2014 - NCIIPC Designated]
    G --> H[2015 - Shreya Singhal Judgment 66A Struck Down]
    H --> I[2018 - Data Protection Bill introduced]
    I --> J[2021 - IT Rules 2021 Social Media OTT]
    J --> K[2021 - Tribunal Reforms Act CyAT abolished]
    K --> L[2022 - CERT-In Directions 28 April]
    L --> M[2023 - DPDP Act 2023 enacted]
    M --> N[2024 onwards - DPDP Rules in force]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Adjudication under the IT Act

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Civil Penalties under Sections 43 / 43A / 44 / 45] --> B[Adjudicating Officer Designation by Central Govt.]
    B --> C[Adjudicating Order]
    C --> D{Appeal to CyAT originally}
    D -->|Pre 2021| E[Cyber Appellate Tribunal]
    D -->|Post 2021| F[Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal TDSAT]
    E --> G[Further Appeal to High Court under Section 62]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Supreme Court of India]

    A2[Criminal Offences under Sections 65 to 78] --> B2[Investigation by Police Officer not below Rank of Inspector]
    B2 --> C2[FIR and Investigation]
    C2 --> D2[Trial by Magistrate]
    D2 --> E2[Session Court if severe]
    E2 --> F2[High Court]
    F2 --> G2[Supreme Court]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Section 66F Cyber Terrorism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Act Defined under Section 66F 1] --> B{Is the act committed with intent to threaten unity integrity security sovereignty or economic health of India}
    B -->|Yes| C[Type of Act]
    C --> D[Denial of access to authorized person]
    C --> E[Introducing malware or virus]
    C --> F[Damage to computer source code]
    C --> G[Denial of critical infrastructure service]

    D --> H[Punishment: Imprisonment up to LIFE]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Trial by Court of Session]
    I --> J[No Bail without hearing Public Prosecutor under Section 66F 2]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question A1]**
> **Q1. List the major objectives of the IT Act, 2008 amendment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The major objectives of the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 are:
1. To bring the law in line with the **UNCITRAL Model Law on Electronic Signatures (2001)** by replacing "digital signature" with the broader "electronic signature."
2. To address new forms of cyber crimes such as **cyber terrorism, identity theft, child pornography, and violation of privacy.**
3. To establish **CERT-In as a statutory authority** under Section 70B for incident response.
4. To provide a **legal framework for data protection** via Section 43A and the SPDI Rules, 2011.
5. To grant **extraterritorial jurisdiction** to Indian courts over cyber offences committed anywhere in the world (Section 75).
6. To introduce an **intermediary safe harbour regime** under Section 79.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question A2]**
> **Q2. Define "Intermediary" and explain the conditions for safe harbour under Section 79 of the IT Act.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition:** As per Section 2(1)(w) of the IT Act, an "intermediary" means any person who on behalf of another person receives, stores or transmits any electronic record or provides any service in respect of that record. Examples include ISPs, telecom operators, web hosting providers, search engines, and online marketplaces.

**Safe Harbour Conditions (Section 79):**
1. The intermediary's role must be limited to **providing access, transmission, hosting, or caching**.
2. The intermediary must **not initiate the transmission**, **not select the receiver**, and **not modify the content**.
3. The intermediary must observe **due diligence** as prescribed under the IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021.
4. Upon receiving actual knowledge or notification by a court or government agency, the intermediary must **act expeditiously** to remove or disable access to the illegal content (within **36 hours** of court order, **72 hours** of government order).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question]**
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **penalties and adjudication mechanism** under Chapter IX of the IT Act, 2008. Discuss the **role of the Adjudicating Officer** and the procedure for **appeal**. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Critically analyse the **landmark *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India* judgment (2015)** with specific reference to Section 66A. Discuss the constitutional grounds and the implications. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### **(a) Penalties and Adjudication Mechanism (7 Marks)**

The **Chapter IX (Sections 43 to 47)** of the IT Act deals with penalties, compensation, and adjudication for civil wrongs in cyberspace.

**Step 1: Types of Penalties (3 Marks)**

| Section | Nature of Penalty | Maximum Amount |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 43** | Damages to computer/computer system | ₹1 crore |
| **Section 43A** | Failure to protect data (body corporate) | ₹5 crore |
| **Section 44** | Failure to furnish documents/returns | ₹1.5 lakh per day |
| **Section 45** | Residuary penalty (any contravention) | ₹25,000 |

**Step 2: Adjudicating Officer (2 Marks)**

- The **Central Government** notifies an officer not below the rank of **Director to the Government of India** (Section 46) as the Adjudicating Officer.
- The Adjudicating Officer has powers of a **Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 — summoning witnesses, discovery, production of documents, etc.
- The officer holds an inquiry and passes a written order determining the penalty.

**Step 3: Appeal Mechanism (2 Marks)**

- **Original Act (Pre-2021):** Aggrieved party could appeal to the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CyAT)** within **45 days** of the order.
- **Post 2021 (Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021):** CyAT was dissolved, and appeals now lie to the **Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT)**.
- Further appeal lies to the **High Court** under the original Section 62 of the IT Act (now Section 17 of the Tribunal Reforms Act).

---

### **(b) Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) — Critical Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Background (2 Marks):**

- Section 66A was inserted by the **2008 Amendment** to penalize the sending of "offensive or menacing" messages through any communication service.
- Punishment: **Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine**.
- The provision was challenged via writ petitions led by **Shreya Singhal** after multiple arrests (e.g., Shaheen Dhada, Rizwan Rehman) for social media posts.

**Constitutional Grounds of Challenge (3 Marks):**

1. **Article 19(1)(a) Violation — Freedom of Speech:** Section 66A imposed a "prior restraint" on the internet, which is a critical medium for the exercise of free speech.
2. **Vagueness Doctrine:** The terms "**grossly offensive**," "**menacing**," "**annoying**," "**insulting**," and "**causing annoyance or inconvenience**" were found to be **vague, subjective, and overbroad** — failing the **"void for vagueness"** test.
3. **Chilling Effect:** The mere existence of the law produced a chilling effect on legitimate online speech, with citizens self-censoring to avoid prosecution.
4. **No Proximate Act Requirement:** Section 66A criminalized speech based purely on content, without requiring any incitement to violence or public disorder.

**Implications of the Judgment (2 Marks):**

1. **Section 66A was struck down** as unconstitutional and unenforceable.
2. **Section 79 was read down:** The "actual knowledge" requirement was interpreted narrowly — intermediaries must be notified by a court order or government notification, not by private complaints.
3. **Section 66A in post-judgment period** — Acts that previously fell under 66A may now be prosecuted under relevant provisions of the **Indian Penal Code (now Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023)** such as Sections 153A, 295A, 505, 507, etc.
4. **Paving the way for *Anuradha Bhasin* (2020)** on the Kashmir internet shutdown and broader digital rights jurisprudence.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question]**
> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Explain the **salient features of the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008** and compare them with the original IT Act, 2000. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **functions, powers, and role of CERT-In** under Section 70B of the IT Act, 2008. Explain the **CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### **(a) Salient Features of the IT (Amendment) Act, 2008 vs. IT Act, 2000 (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Original IT Act, 2000 (2 Marks)**

The IT Act, 2000 was the **first cyber law in India** focused primarily on:
- Legal recognition of **electronic records** and **digital signatures**.
- Facilitation of **e-commerce** and **e-governance**.
- Regulation of **Certifying Authorities (CAs)**.
- Penalties for limited cyber crimes (hacking under Section 66, source code tampering under Section 65).

**Step 2: Salient Features Added by 2008 Amendment (3 Marks)**

1. **Recognition of "Electronic Signature":** Replaced narrow "digital signature" with broader "electronic signature" to align with UNCITRAL Model Law.
2. **New Criminal Offences:**
   - Section 66A (later struck down): Offensive messages
   - Section 66B: Dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource
   - Section 66C: Identity theft
   - Section 66D: Cheating by personation
   - Section 66E: Violation of privacy
   - Section 66F: **Cyber terrorism** (life imprisonment)
   - Section 67A: Sexually explicit material
   - Section 67B: **Child pornography**
3. **Statutory Bodies:**
   - **Section 70A:** National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (**NCIIPC**)
   - **Section 70B:** **CERT-In** with statutory backing
4. **Intermediary Safe Harbour (Section 79):** New regime for protecting intermediaries.
5. **Data Protection (Section 43A):** Liability of body corporates for data breach.
6. **Interception Powers (Section 69 + 69A + 69B):** Comprehensive framework.
7. **Extraterritorial Jurisdiction (Section 75):** Indian courts can try cyber offences by foreigners against Indian citizens or on Indian computer resources.

**Step 3: Comparative Table (2 Marks)**

[Refer to the Comparative Analysis table in Section 3.5 above — students can present a 6-row comparison.]

---

### **(b) CERT-In — Functions, Powers, and 2022 Directions (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Genesis and Statutory Status (2 Marks)**

- CERT-In was established in **2004** as a functional organization under the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY).
- After the **2008 Amendment**, it was given **statutory status under Section 70B** as the **national nodal agency** for cybersecurity incident response.
- It is headquartered in **New Delhi**.

**Step 2: Functions of CERT-In under Section 70B(4) (3 Marks)**

1. **Information Collection:** Collect, analyse, and disseminate information on cyber incidents.
2. **Forecasting & Alerts:** Forecast and alert on cybersecurity incidents; provide advisories.
3. **Emergency Measures:** Take emergency measures to handle cyber security incidents.
4. **Coordination:** Coordinate incident response among affected entities.
5. **Guidelines:** Issue guidelines, advisories, and vulnerability notes.
6. **Other Functions:** Such other functions as may be prescribed.

**Step 3: CERT-In Directions of 28 April 2022 (2 Marks)**

Under Section 70B(6), CERT-In issued binding directions for cybersecurity:

1. **6-Hour Reporting Mandate:** All cybersecurity incidents must be reported to CERT-In within **6 hours** of detection.
2. **Log Retention:** All service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates must synchronize and maintain logs for **180 days**.
3. **Subscriber KYC:** Data centres, cloud providers, VPN service providers, and crypto exchanges must register and maintain KYC/ownership records of subscribers for **5 years**.
4. **Validation of Customers:** All companies selling SIM cards, registering VPN services, or selling crypto must maintain validated customer details.
5. **Penalty for Non-Compliance:** Failure to comply attracts imprisonment up to **1 year** or fine.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "IT Act, 2008" with "IT Act, 2000":** Always clarify that the **parent Act is 2000**, and the **2008 Amendment** is what students colloquially refer to as "IT Act, 2008." Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Mentioning Section 66A as valid law:** Section 66A was **STRUCK DOWN** in 2015. Never state it is enforceable. Examiners deduct full marks for this error.
> 3. **Confusing CERT-In with NCIIPC:** CERT-In handles **incident response** (Section 70B), whereas NCIIPC handles **Critical Information Infrastructure protection** (Section 70A). Do not mix them up.
> 4. **Confusing Penalty with Offence:** A penalty is **civil** (Adjudicating Officer), and an offence is **criminal** (Magistrate/Court). Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **CyAT vs. TDSAT confusion:** The Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CyAT) was **dissolved in 2021** under the Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021. Appeals now lie with **TDSAT**. Stating CyAT as the current appellate body is incorrect.
> 6. **Digital Signature vs. Electronic Signature:** The 2008 Amendment broadened the concept. Use "**Electronic Signature**" for the 2008+ regime, not "Digital Signature" only.
> 7. **Missing out on the 2015 *Shreya Singhal* judgment:** This is a **favourite 7-mark question** for KTU valuations. Always mention the case name and the year.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Parent Act:** IT Act, **2000** (enacted 9 June 2000). **Amendment:** IT (Amendment) Act, **2008** (notified 27 October 2009).
> - **Sections in total:** ~94 sections across 13 chapters and 4 schedules.
> - **Section 43** = Civil damage (₹1 crore). **Section 43A** = Data protection (₹5 crore).
> - **Section 66A** = **STUCK DOWN** in *Shreya Singhal v. UoI* (2015) — must be remembered.
> - **Section 66F** = **Cyber terrorism** = Life imprisonment.
> - **Section 67** = Obscene material in electronic form. **Section 67B** = Child pornography.
> - **Section 70B** = **CERT-In** (national nodal agency for cyber incident response).
> - **Section 70A** = **NCIIPC** (Critical Information Infrastructure).
> - **Section 75** = **Extraterritorial jurisdiction** — universal applicability of the Act.
> - **Section 79** = Intermediary **safe harbour** — applicable if due diligence followed.
> - **Section 81** = Act has **overriding effect** over other laws in case of conflict.
> - **Adjudicating Officer** = Civil penalty adjudicator (not below Director rank).
> - **Appellate Body** = Originally CyAT, now **TDSAT** (post-2021 Tribunal Reforms Act).
> - **CERT-In Directions (28 April 2022):** 6-hour incident reporting, 180-day log retention, 5-year subscriber record retention.
> - **Section 77:** Payment of compensation/penalty does **NOT** bar criminal prosecution (concurrent liability).
> - **DPDP Act, 2023:** The future of data protection in India — based on the **Puttaswamy judgment (2017)** right to privacy.
> - **Penalty ≠ Offence:** Civil vs. Criminal — know the difference.
> - **Budapest Convention** on Cybercrime — India is **NOT** a signatory.
> - **UNCITRAL Model Law on Electronic Signatures (2001)** — the 2008 Amendment aligns with this.
> - **Cyber Café Rules, 2011** — log maintenance, registration with state government.
> - **SPDI Rules, 2011** — Sensitive Personal Data or Information rules under Section 43A.
> - **IT Rules, 2021 (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code)** — applicable to social media intermediaries, OTT platforms, digital news media.
> - **Critical Information Infrastructure (CII):** Computer resource whose destruction would have a debilitating impact on national security, economy, public health, or safety — protected under Section 70.
> - **Section 69:** Interception, monitoring, decryption by government agencies; **Section 69A:** Blocking of websites; **Section 69B:** Collection of traffic/metadata.

**End of Module 4 Notes — IT Act, 2008 and Its Amendments**
